title: Cushing's Syndrome
oneLiner: High cortisol state (endogenous or iatrogenic) causing metabolic syndrome, immunosuppression, and hypercoagulability. Commonest from exogenous steroids.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Adrenal crisis (if stopping high-dose steroids abruptly):** Refractory hypotension, hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia.  
  - *Key finding:* hypotension not responding to fluids + history of steroid use.
- **Pulmonary embolism:** Sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia.  
  - *Key finding:* hypercoagulable state (cortisol excess) + clinical suspicion.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Central obesity with proximal myopathy (difficulty rising from chair)
  - Easy bruising, purple striae (usually >1 cm, on abdomen/breasts)
  - Hirsutism, oligomenorrhoea/amenorrhoea, erectile dysfunction
  - Depression, emotional lability, psychosis
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Moon face, buffalo hump, supraclavicular fat pads
  - Thin, fragile skin with petechiae/purpura
  - Proximal muscle weakness (test: squat stand, arm raise against resistance)
  - Hypertension, peripheral oedema
  - Acne, hirsutism (women), gynaecomastia (men)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Exogenous/iatrogenic (most common)** – History of steroid use (oral, inhaled, topical, injections)
- **Cushing's disease (pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma)** – High ACTH, female:male 3:1, slower onset
- **Ectopic ACTH secretion (e.g. small cell lung cancer, carcinoid)** – Very high ACTH, rapid onset, severe hypokalaemia, often older/smoker
- **Adrenal adenoma/carcinoma** – Low ACTH, may present with virilisation (adrenal carcinoma)
- **Pseudo-Cushing's (alcohol, depression, obesity)** – Mild features, normal overnight dexamethasone test
- **Splitter:** Plasma ACTH level  
  - High → pituitary or ectopic  
  - Low/suppressed → adrenal or exogenous
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dip for glucose (high yield), BP lying and standing
  - ECG: look for LVH (chronic hypertension) or QTc prolongation (hypokalaemia)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E (hypokalaemia common in ectopic), glucose (fasting), HbA1c
  - 8am cortisol and ACTH (paired)
  - 24h urinary free cortisol (UFC) – gold standard first-line
  - Dexamethasone suppression tests (see special tests)
  - DHEAS (adrenal androgens) – high in adrenal carcinoma
- **Imaging:**
  - Low ACTH → CT adrenals (unenhanced + contrast)
  - High ACTH → MRI pituitary (contrast-enhanced, 3T)
  - If ectopic suspected → CT chest ± abdomen ± PET-CT (e.g. for carcinoid)
- **Special tests:**
  - **1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test:** Give 1mg dexamethasone at 23:00; measure 8am cortisol. Cut-off: <50 nmol/l excludes Cushing's. Value >50 nmol/l suggests Cushing's.
  - **CRH test:** After dexamethasone suppression, give CRH; cortisol >50 nmol/l confirms Cushing's disease.
  - **Inferior petrosal sinus sampling:** For ACTH-dependent disease when MRI negative.
- **Never-miss test:** Exclude exogenous steroids by direct questioning (including inhaled, topical, intra-articular, eye drops). Missing this leads to inappropriate further testing.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess ABC, check BP, glucose, ECG.
  - If hypertensive urgency (BP >180/110 with headache, visual change, or chest pain): treat with IV labetalol 20mg over 2 min, repeat if needed – or oral amlodipine 5mg – [check local policy].
  - If severe hyperglycaemia (>15 mmol/l): start insulin sliding scale.
  - If suspected infection: cultures (blood, urine, sputum) + broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam per local guidance).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis (dexamethasone test, ACTH, imaging).
  - Refer endocrinology for guidance.
  - Medical control if symptomatic: metyrapone 250mg PO 6-hourly, adjust to cortisol (aim mid-range 150-300 nmol/l) – [check local protocol].
  - Monitor daily weight, fluid balance, electrolytes (K+), and blood glucose.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily if admitted [check weight/renal dose].
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Definitive: if pituitary – transsphenoidal surgery; if adrenal – laparoscopic adrenalectomy; if ectopic – treat underlying tumour.
  - Post-surgery: glucocorticoid replacement (hydrocortisone 15-25mg daily in divided doses) until HPA axis recovery.
  - Radiotherapy to pituitary if incomplete resection.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: only if hypotensive – 0.9% saline at 125ml/hr, monitor fluid overload (cardiac risk).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjust if eGFR <30).
  - Diet: low sodium, low simple sugar, high protein.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly unless unstable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Metyrapone:** 250mg PO 6-hourly, titrate up to 500mg 6-hourly – monitor cortisol levels. Do not start without endocrinology advice unless emergency.
  - **Ketoconazole:** 200mg PO twice daily, increase to 400mg twice daily – monitor LFTs weekly.
  - **Hydrocortisone (for adrenal crisis or post-surgery):** 100mg IV stat then 50-100mg IV 6-hourly, taper to oral over 2-3 days.
- **Common alternatives:** Osilodrostat (specialist use only), etomidate (IV ICU use for rapid control).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Ketoconazole: hepatotoxic; avoid in liver impairment; interacts with many drugs (statins, warfarin, etc.)
  - Metyrapone: can cause hirsutism (androgen effect); avoid in pregnancy.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Ketoconazole – reduce dose in hepatic impairment; no renal adjustment needed. Metyrapone – no dose change needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Over-suppression of cortisol causing adrenal insufficiency – present with hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia. Always have IV hydrocortisone available. Never start metyrapone without a clear plan for monitoring and rescue steroids.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (if overtreated or stressors)
  - Severe hypertension (may precipitate stroke)
  - Hyperglycaemic emergencies (DKA / HHS)
  - Thromboembolism (DVT/PE)
  - Acute psychosis
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis (accelerated bone loss)
  - Type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia
  - Cardiovascular disease (LVH, coronary artery disease)
  - Recurrent infections (immunosuppression)
  - Proximal myopathy (persistent weakness)
  - Poor wound healing, skin fragility
sections.Prognosis:
- Good if cause identified and treated; surgical cure rate ~70-90% for pituitary adenoma.
- Without treatment: 50% 5-year mortality (CV disease, infections).
- Post-surgery most require lifelong glucocorticoid replacement; HPA axis recovery may take 6-18 months.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- 1) Always ask about ALL exogenous steroid sources (including creams, inhalers, joint injections) – this is the commonest cause.
- 2) Use 24h urinary free cortisol and 1mg overnight dexamethasone test before imaging – they are the diagnostic backbone.
- 3) In ectopic ACTH: suspect if rapid onset, severe hypokalaemia, and high ACTH – then image chest first (CT thorax for small cell lung cancer).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- 1) Do not rely on a single random cortisol level – it can be normal in mild disease.
- 2) Do not start medical therapy (metyrapone/ketoconazole) before localisation of cause if stable – it can mask the source.
- 3) Do not abruptly stop steroids in iatrogenic Cushing's – taper slowly (e.g. reduce prednisolone by 2.5mg every 1-2 weeks) to avoid adrenal crisis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Severe weakness, dizziness, vomiting, or collapse (possible adrenal insufficiency)
  - Signs of infection (fever, rigors, new cough, dysuria)
  - Severe headache, vision changes, chest pain, or palpitations
- **Follow-up:** Endocrinology clinic within 4 weeks; if osteoporosis risk, dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) scan.

rawMarkdown:
## Cushing's Syndrome
**One-liner:** High cortisol state (endogenous or iatrogenic) causing metabolic syndrome, immunosuppression, and hypercoagulability. Commonest from exogenous steroids.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Adrenal crisis (if stopping high-dose steroids abruptly):** Refractory hypotension, hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia.  
  - *Key finding:* hypotension not responding to fluids + history of steroid use.
- **Pulmonary embolism:** Sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia.  
  - *Key finding:* hypercoagulable state (cortisol excess) + clinical suspicion.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Central obesity with proximal myopathy (difficulty rising from chair)
  - Easy bruising, purple striae (usually >1 cm, on abdomen/breasts)
  - Hirsutism, oligomenorrhoea/amenorrhoea, erectile dysfunction
  - Depression, emotional lability, psychosis
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Moon face, buffalo hump, supraclavicular fat pads
  - Thin, fragile skin with petechiae/purpura
  - Proximal muscle weakness (test: squat stand, arm raise against resistance)
  - Hypertension, peripheral oedema
  - Acne, hirsutism (women), gynaecomastia (men)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Exogenous/iatrogenic (most common)** – History of steroid use (oral, inhaled, topical, injections)
- **Cushing's disease (pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma)** – High ACTH, female:male 3:1, slower onset
- **Ectopic ACTH secretion (e.g. small cell lung cancer, carcinoid)** – Very high ACTH, rapid onset, severe hypokalaemia, often older/smoker
- **Adrenal adenoma/carcinoma** – Low ACTH, may present with virilisation (adrenal carcinoma)
- **Pseudo-Cushing's (alcohol, depression, obesity)** – Mild features, normal overnight dexamethasone test
- **Splitter:** Plasma ACTH level  
  - High → pituitary or ectopic  
  - Low/suppressed → adrenal or exogenous

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dip for glucose (high yield), BP lying and standing
  - ECG: look for LVH (chronic hypertension) or QTc prolongation (hypokalaemia)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E (hypokalaemia common in ectopic), glucose (fasting), HbA1c
  - 8am cortisol and ACTH (paired)
  - 24h urinary free cortisol (UFC) – gold standard first-line
  - Dexamethasone suppression tests (see special tests)
  - DHEAS (adrenal androgens) – high in adrenal carcinoma
- **Imaging:**
  - Low ACTH → CT adrenals (unenhanced + contrast)
  - High ACTH → MRI pituitary (contrast-enhanced, 3T)
  - If ectopic suspected → CT chest ± abdomen ± PET-CT (e.g. for carcinoid)
- **Special tests:**
  - **1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test:** Give 1mg dexamethasone at 23:00; measure 8am cortisol. Cut-off: <50 nmol/l excludes Cushing's. Value >50 nmol/l suggests Cushing's.
  - **CRH test:** After dexamethasone suppression, give CRH; cortisol >50 nmol/l confirms Cushing's disease.
  - **Inferior petrosal sinus sampling:** For ACTH-dependent disease when MRI negative.
- **Never-miss test:** Exclude exogenous steroids by direct questioning (including inhaled, topical, intra-articular, eye drops). Missing this leads to inappropriate further testing.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess ABC, check BP, glucose, ECG.
  - If hypertensive urgency (BP >180/110 with headache, visual change, or chest pain): treat with IV labetalol 20mg over 2 min, repeat if needed – or oral amlodipine 5mg – [check local policy].
  - If severe hyperglycaemia (>15 mmol/l): start insulin sliding scale.
  - If suspected infection: cultures (blood, urine, sputum) + broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam per local guidance).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis (dexamethasone test, ACTH, imaging).
  - Refer endocrinology for guidance.
  - Medical control if symptomatic: metyrapone 250mg PO 6-hourly, adjust to cortisol (aim mid-range 150-300 nmol/l) – [check local protocol].
  - Monitor daily weight, fluid balance, electrolytes (K+), and blood glucose.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily if admitted [check weight/renal dose].
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Definitive: if pituitary – transsphenoidal surgery; if adrenal – laparoscopic adrenalectomy; if ectopic – treat underlying tumour.
  - Post-surgery: glucocorticoid replacement (hydrocortisone 15-25mg daily in divided doses) until HPA axis recovery.
  - Radiotherapy to pituitary if incomplete resection.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: only if hypotensive – 0.9% saline at 125ml/hr, monitor fluid overload (cardiac risk).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjust if eGFR <30).
  - Diet: low sodium, low simple sugar, high protein.
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly unless unstable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Metyrapone:** 250mg PO 6-hourly, titrate up to 500mg 6-hourly – monitor cortisol levels. Do not start without endocrinology advice unless emergency.
  - **Ketoconazole:** 200mg PO twice daily, increase to 400mg twice daily – monitor LFTs weekly.
  - **Hydrocortisone (for adrenal crisis or post-surgery):** 100mg IV stat then 50-100mg IV 6-hourly, taper to oral over 2-3 days.
- **Common alternatives:** Osilodrostat (specialist use only), etomidate (IV ICU use for rapid control).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Ketoconazole: hepatotoxic; avoid in liver impairment; interacts with many drugs (statins, warfarin, etc.)
  - Metyrapone: can cause hirsutism (androgen effect); avoid in pregnancy.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Ketoconazole – reduce dose in hepatic impairment; no renal adjustment needed. Metyrapone – no dose change needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Over-suppression of cortisol causing adrenal insufficiency – present with hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia. Always have IV hydrocortisone available. Never start metyrapone without a clear plan for monitoring and rescue steroids.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (if overtreated or stressors)
  - Severe hypertension (may precipitate stroke)
  - Hyperglycaemic emergencies (DKA / HHS)
  - Thromboembolism (DVT/PE)
  - Acute psychosis
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis (accelerated bone loss)
  - Type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia
  - Cardiovascular disease (LVH, coronary artery disease)
  - Recurrent infections (immunosuppression)
  - Proximal myopathy (persistent weakness)
  - Poor wound healing, skin fragility

### Prognosis
- Good if cause identified and treated; surgical cure rate ~70-90% for pituitary adenoma.
- Without treatment: 50% 5-year mortality (CV disease, infections).
- Post-surgery most require lifelong glucocorticoid replacement; HPA axis recovery may take 6-18 months.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- 1) Always ask about ALL exogenous steroid sources (including creams, inhalers, joint injections) – this is the commonest cause.
- 2) Use 24h urinary free cortisol and 1mg overnight dexamethasone test before imaging – they are the diagnostic backbone.
- 3) In ectopic ACTH: suspect if rapid onset, severe hypokalaemia, and high ACTH – then image chest first (CT thorax for small cell lung cancer).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- 1) Do not rely on a single random cortisol level – it can be normal in mild disease.
- 2) Do not start medical therapy (metyrapone/ketoconazole) before localisation of cause if stable – it can mask the source.
- 3) Do not abruptly stop steroids in iatrogenic Cushing's – taper slowly (e.g. reduce prednisolone by 2.5mg every 1-2 weeks) to avoid adrenal crisis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Severe weakness, dizziness, vomiting, or collapse (possible adrenal insufficiency)
  - Signs of infection (fever, rigors, new cough, dysuria)
  - Severe headache, vision changes, chest pain, or palpitations
- **Follow-up:** Endocrinology clinic within 4 weeks; if osteoporosis risk, dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) scan.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate >2 mmol/l
